memberdef.definition: static std::vector< T_Inner > JSL::String::internal::Converter< std::vector< T_Inner > >::internalConvert
memberdef.name: internalConvert

briefdescription.para: Calls internalConvert(std::string_view, std::string_view,typename) with the default delimiter (a comma)

memberdef.definition: static std::vector< T_Inner > JSL::String::internal::Converter< std::vector< T_Inner > >::internalConvert
memberdef.argsstring: (std::string_view sv, std::string_view element_delimiter, typename std::enable_if_t<!std::is_same_v< T_Inner, char > > *=nullptr)
memberdef.name: internalConvert

briefdescription.para: The iterative converter for vector types loops over the split-vector and converts the internal types.

detaileddescription.para: The typename argument allows the function to disable itself at compile time if T_Inner is a char, thus inducing a Substitution Failure. Through SFINAE principles, this allows std::string (internally a std::vector<char> in many ways) to be ignored by this, and thus picked up by the standard converter

inbodydescription.para: We allow vectors to be wrapped in either [], {} or (). This function removes them for internal use.

simplesect.para: We do not do any form of parsing or checking to allow nested braces. End caps are purely for user readability.

detaileddescription.para: For reasons unknown to me, Apple-Clang does not fully implement the C++ standard. std::from_chars does not work on non-integral types. This is really, really annoying that this is necessary. Must therefore resort to using the (slower, worse) std::stod for Apple people
A partial specialisation of Converter<T> to allow the extraction of vectors.
Functionally, this acts as a wrapper, iteratively calling Converter<T_Inner> on the values extracted
